Alright, JB is in the house for your bonus lesson on scripting, but I gotta tell you, it's really not a bonus lesson. This is a real workshop lesson here of the highest order. You're gonna love this. This is me talking to you casually at my desk here, a bit slower, and I'm really gonna take you by the hand and take you through this process of constructing a world-class straight line script that creates that screen from the hilltops, airtight logical case that is so important in getting to the point where you can close anyone who's closeable. Now remember, people don't buy on logic, they buy on emotion and justify the decisions with logic. We have to get the emotional patterns in place as well, but we always start with logic because what we do is we create this incredibly airtight logical case which then frees someone up, disarms them, the human bullshit detects it, so to speak, and say, wow, this makes sense, everything connects from A to Z, A to Z, tell you what you're part of the world, right? A to Z, A to Z, right? Point being is that once someone's like, wow, this really makes sense, I'm a buy-in, I really like this though, wow, it makes sense, you are really close to closing that deal because then all you have to do is then move them emotionally and you're done. And that's what your looping patterns are all about. So with that, a couple of things. Number one, we're not gonna go through looping patterns here. I'm gonna save that for the looping module which is coming soon. Okay, we have one stop in the middle, I'm gonna go through standards first and I'll tell you why in a few minutes here, but we're gonna be at looping soon enough. So in this workshop right here, I'm gonna take you through that first word out of your mouth and then take you all the way to the point where you ask for the order the first time and then we'll save the looping patterns and upsells and cross-sells for that module coming soon to a learning management system near you, this one, all right? So here's the deal. What we're going to do is I'm gonna take you through this guided exercise here from the first word out of your mouth going all the way down to the close which is when you ask for the order for the first time. Now while that might seem like an overwhelming effort, do all that, it's not because we're gonna break it down into these small bite-sized chunks, that's the ticket. Now one important point here, remember one of the key principles of a straight line script is that we want to frame and not front load. However, that being said, in this first evolution, don't worry about that, I want you to front load away. I don't care how many words it is, I don't care if it would take you, you know, two hours to make it through a sales presentation, that's okay. The perfect metaphor for this is a statue. Imagine you start with this raw slab of marble and you want to carve out the beautiful statue of David. Well, what do you do? You start off with a big block and you slowly chip away and you mold what you don't want to do is start off and chip away down to the core and try to get it perfect the first time because guess what? If you over chip, you over trim, you can't start again, you can't build it back up again. You get it? So what we're gonna do here is start by writing a long front-loaded script from the first word of your open, going straight to the close, not past that because again, we'll handle that in the looping section and then after you do that, we'll then start whittling it down step by step to get you the perfect straight line script. Make sense? Okay, great. Now, that process obviously starts with the introduction. In other words, how do you introduce some? What are those words that you say in those first four seconds, which are those all important first four seconds, right? Because remember, while it's not the words that really matter in terms of what moves something emotionally and establishes you as an expert sharp on the ball, the words have to be right. So your introduction starts with those first few words out of your mouth and it runs about in 10, 15, 20 seconds to the point where you start asking your intelligence gathering questions and that might run maybe tops 30 seconds at most, but probably more like 20 seconds or less, all right? So here's the deal. There's going to be a fundamental difference in what these words are if you're selling over the phone versus in person. So what I want to do now is divide you up into groups and we'll do this into three groups for those three possibilities, right? Possibility number one, are you doing your sales strictly over the telephone? Is that who you are? Is that what you're doing? Or are you going out into the field where you're selling face to face or same thing is all you're going to store, card, doze, whatever it might be, what people are coming to you, so it's a face to face sale or third possibility, are you in a hybrid situation where you have encounters that are both in person and over the phone depending where you are in the sale cycle or you just might sell both ways? Pick one of those three right now because based on what you pick, there'll be a different training. All right, perfect. You're in a hybrid situation. You're going out into the field and closing sales and doing it on the phone. Love it, all right? So let's go through how to construct and deliver awesome world-class straight line scripts for both and let's start with over the telephone first. It'll make sense. You'll see why in a moment. So here's the first thing you need to understand. The key to constructing a successful script is going to be to actually go the whole way through with every single word you say, being a word for word match for what's going to be written. In other words, I want you to get this script to the point where the words are so tight. Like I give you an example, like I said in the training, I said that with Leo de Caprio in the movie, I said, you know, you know, name of the company, arrow tie international. Not it. Now the name of the company is, it was name of the company, not the name, name of the company arrow tie. That's a boom, boom power, right? If you're selling on the phone, the script that we're going to write is going to actually have to be that accurate, meaning that every word that's on the script is going to be said on the phone. You have to drop certain words on your script, make certain contractions, break all the grammar rules that you currently know. We're going to have half thoughts, dangling sentences, you get it? That's how you write a perfect phone script. Okay? So again, if that's who you are, well, guess what? You're going to have to take the time now to practice and drill with the script, practice and drill, practice and drill and know that your first few pitches, you know, if I say up to the first five to 10, I'm not going to work perfectly. You're going to have to actually go through it, see what's working, what's not, what's should be too much of, you know, a tongue twister. Usually also what you're going to find mostly is that you tend to ramble on a bit when you, you know, actually write out script as much as we define it by carving out and carving out to get that perfect statue we want. It's still going to probably be too long. You're going to need some nips and tucks. Just be prepared that when you use it in the first time, when you're out there on the actual phone, it's not going to probably work as well as you'd like. But all you're going to do is you say, okay, hang up next time you make a call, you're already trimmed it, you went through another evolution. Under 10 times, you'll have a perfect script that is that on target, literally word for word, the whole way through. You get it? Now in terms of how you then take that script and bring it out into the field, well, obviously you're not going to come up very professional if you walk up to someone's door into their office and say, hi, my name is Jordan Delford. They might, it's not going to work at all, right? So what you're going to do here is we're going to go through an exercise where we take that script that's already been written and been honed to near perfection through these evolutions of it's been trimmed down and trimmed down so every word is perfect. And now we're actually going to have to make it sort of imperfect in the sense that you take that beautiful, elegant, perfectly worded statement and we're going to divide it now into these blocks of thoughts. In other words, it'll be a block. What's your opening? Block what's your transition? Block thought. What's your main body? And before that, I missed one, block what are your intelligence gathering questions? You get it? In other words, there's going to be a bit of memorization to do here. But what we want here are these perfect anchor points. So you always know, okay, I got these first 15 or 20 words memorized and that is really easy to do. It's not hard, a little practice and repetition, practice and repetition. And you know that even though you don't have your actual script right in front of you, well, you really are as good as having your script in front of you because frankly, anyway, anyone can memorize a couple of sentences and that's all it really takes. These are language patterns. They're sound bites. They're short. They're concise, but they're powerful. So all you have to do now is we take that script and say, okay, what's our best opening sound bite? What's our best transitional sound bite? And then start asking our intelligence gathering questions. What's our best transitional sound bite that takes us from that to what? The main body of our presentations. And then what's our best sound bite at the end? Like that pattern of creating urgency, following all the rules we learned in straight line scripting, right? You have all of these rules now. Yes, you're out in the field and you can't have a script, right? But in most industries, you can very easily use sales aids strategically. In other words, in the mortgage industry, I'd show up to someone's door and have a little checklist. Like, yeah, oh, what rate are you paying? Oh, really? Like a doctor, when he does his exam, we understand, we've been conditioned to accept the fact that people with true experts are going to have a form or a list they're going through, because they want to be thorough. Obviously, your body language has to be correct. You can be like, hold on a second, what's the number? That's not good. But if you're like, uh-huh, uh-huh, mm-hmm, ooh. You can use these sales aids. You keep using it and then whittling it down. Using it, whittling it down. You first do some role playing. So you actually, what are the tongue twisters? What doesn't flow correctly? What's too wordy? They'll all tend to be too wordy in the beginning. And then you bring it out into the world live and then again you'll have to start whittling it down.
